verses.text: Now the Philistines fought against Israel; and the men of Israel fled from before the Philistines, and fell down slain in mount Gilboa. And the Philistines followed hard after Saul, and after his sons; and the Philistines slew Jonathan, and Abinadab, and Malchishua, the sons of Saul. And the battle went sore against Saul, and the archers hit him, and he was wounded of the archers. Then said Saul to his armourbearer, Draw thy sword, and thrust me through therewith; lest these uncircumcised come and abuse me. But his armourbearer would not; for he was sore afraid. So Saul took a sword, and fell upon it. And when his armourbearer saw that Saul was dead, he fell likewise on the sword, and died. So Saul died, and his three sons, and all his house died together. And when all the men of Israel that were in the valley saw that they fled, and that Saul and his sons were dead, then they forsook their cities, and fled: and the Philistines came and dwelt in them. Guidance: After nine chapters of genealogy, the narrative of 1 Chronicles opens on a battlefield of defeat and death. Israel flees before the Philistines; Saul's three sons — including noble Jonathan — are slain; Saul himself, wounded by archers and dreading capture, falls upon his own sword; his armourbearer follows him in death. 'So Saul died, and his three sons, and all his house died together.' It is a scene of utter collapse: the king dead, his heirs dead, the army shattered, the cities abandoned to the enemy. The chapter is showing us the tragic end of Israel's first king — a man who began with such promise, head and shoulders above the people, anointed by Samuel, but who turned from the LORD and now dies in disgrace and despair on Mount Gilboa. The chapter is teaching us the bitter end of a life that departs from God: Saul's reign, which started in the Spirit's power, ends in suicide and ruin, his whole house swept away in a single day. The chapter is teaching us that no natural gifts, no high position, no early anointing can preserve a man who abandons obedience to the LORD; the king who would not submit to God fell at last before his enemies. The death of Jonathan, faithful and beloved, in the same battle reminds us that the consequences of a leader's unfaithfulness fall also on those around him — even the godly Jonathan perished in his father's defeat. The chapter is teaching us to take warning from Saul: that the way of disobedience, however long the LORD's patience, leads finally to darkness. And it sets the stage, by clearing it of Saul's house, for the rise of the king the LORD had chosen after His own heart.

And it came to pass on the morrow, when the Philistines came to strip the slain, that they found Saul and his sons fallen in mount Gilboa. And when they had stripped him, they took his head, and his armour, and sent into the land of the Philistines round about, to carry tidings unto their idols, and to the people. And they put his armour in the house of their gods, and fastened his head in the temple of Dagon. And when all Jabeshgilead heard all that the Philistines had done to Saul, They arose, all the valiant men, and took away the body of Saul, and the bodies of his sons, and brought them to Jabesh, and buried their bones under the oak in Jabesh, and fasted seven days. Guidance: The morning after the battle brings fresh shame: the Philistines strip Saul's body, cut off his head, and display his armour in the house of their gods, fastening his head in the temple of Dagon — turning the death of Israel's king into a triumph for their idols. Yet against this dark scene shines a single act of brave loyalty: the valiant men of Jabesh-gilead, hearing of it, travel through the night, recover the bodies of Saul and his sons, bury them honourably, and fast seven days. The chapter is showing us two responses to Saul's fall — the gloating desecration of the pagans, who credited their idol Dagon with the victory, and the grateful devotion of Jabesh-gilead, who remembered that Saul had once delivered their city in his better days (1 Sam 11) and would not leave his body to dishonour. The chapter is teaching us that even at the lowest moment, acts of loyalty and gratitude can shine; the men of Jabesh repaid an old kindness with courage and reverence, honouring the LORD's anointed even in his disgraceful death. The chapter is teaching us the worth of gratitude and faithfulness — these valiant men risked themselves to do right by Saul because he had once done right by them. The chapter is teaching us, too, the emptiness of the pagans' boast: they fastened Saul's head in Dagon's temple as though their idol had triumphed, yet Dagon was no god, and the true reason for Saul's fall lay not in Philistine strength or Dagon's power but, as the next verses declare, in Saul's sin against the LORD. The chapter is teaching us to render kindness for kindness and honour where honour is due, even as it exposes the hollow triumph of those who credit their idols for what the LORD alone ordains.

So Saul died for his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not, and also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit, to enquire of it; And enquired not of the LORD: therefore he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse. Guidance: Here the Chronicler gives his own inspired verdict on the whole tragedy — the theological heart of the chapter, and the reason he told the story at all. Saul died, he says, for three connected sins: 'his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not' (his repeated disobedience, as in the matter of Amalek and the unlawful sacrifice); 'also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit' (his desperate visit to the medium of Endor); and crowning all, that he 'enquired not of the LORD.' Therefore, the Chronicler concludes, 'he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse.' The chapter is showing us that Saul's death was not merely a military defeat but a divine judgment — the LORD Himself slew him for persistent rebellion and for seeking guidance from the dead rather than from God. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of disobedience to God's word: Saul's fundamental sin was that he 'kept not' the word of the LORD; a life of self-will, however religious in appearance, is rebellion against God. The chapter is teaching us the folly and wickedness of seeking help from forbidden sources — Saul turned to a medium because he had cut himself off from God by his own sin, and consulting the spirits sealed his condemnation. But supremely, the chapter is teaching us the contrast at the book's foundation: Saul 'enquired not of the LORD.' This single phrase exposes the difference between the rejected king and the king to come, for David's whole life was marked by enquiring of the LORD — seeking His will, asking before he acted, depending on God. The kingdom was turned to David not by accident or human politics but by the sovereign LORD, who removes one king and raises up another according to His purpose. The chapter is teaching us the lesson that governs all of Chronicles and all of life: to seek the LORD is the way of blessing, and to forsake His word and refuse to enquire of Him is the way of ruin. Saul's epitaph is a warning written large — and the turning of the kingdom to David is the dawn of hope, the rise of the man after God's own heart, through whose line the true and faithful King would come.

So Saul died for his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not, and also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit, to enquire of it; And enquired not of the LORD: therefore he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse. Guidance: Here the Chronicler gives his own inspired verdict on the whole tragedy — the theological heart of the chapter, and the reason he told the story at all. Saul died, he says, for three connected sins: 'his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not' (his repeated disobedience, as in the matter of Amalek and the unlawful sacrifice); 'also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit' (his desperate visit to the medium of Endor); and crowning all, that he 'enquired not of the LORD.' Therefore, the Chronicler concludes, 'he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse.' The chapter is showing us that Saul's death was not merely a military defeat but a divine judgment — the LORD Himself slew him for persistent rebellion and for seeking guidance from the dead rather than from God. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of disobedience to God's word: Saul's fundamental sin was that he 'kept not' the word of the LORD; a life of self-will, however religious in appearance, is rebellion against God. The chapter is teaching us the folly and wickedness of seeking help from forbidden sources — Saul turned to a medium because he had cut himself off from God by his own sin, and consulting the spirits sealed his condemnation. But supremely, the chapter is teaching us the contrast at the book's foundation: Saul 'enquired not of the LORD.' This single phrase exposes the difference between the rejected king and the king to come, for David's whole life was marked by enquiring of the LORD — seeking His will, asking before he acted, depending on God. The kingdom was turned to David not by accident or human politics but by the sovereign LORD, who removes one king and raises up another according to His purpose. The chapter is teaching us the lesson that governs all of Chronicles and all of life: to seek the LORD is the way of blessing, and to forsake His word and refuse to enquire of Him is the way of ruin. Saul's epitaph is a warning written large — and the turning of the kingdom to David is the dawn of hope, the rise of the man after God's own heart, through whose line the true and faithful King would come.
